# Questions in Livestream 161

Live Stream #161: All The Second-Born Questions

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=961" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:01</a> | When was Amenhotep II born? | He says there is no direct birth notice and reasons instead from the mummy's estimated age and the span of his reign. | He estimates Amenhotep II's birth indirectly: the mummy is about fifty-two years old, he reigned about twenty-five or twenty-six years, and he came to the throne around 1425 BC at roughly eighteen. That calculation puts his birth around 1443 BC, which Dr. Falk notes would be about three years after the early Exodus date. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=1219" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:19</a> | Why is the city of palms listed between the Negev and Zoar? | He says Deuteronomy 34 is not a straight-line itinerary but a sweeping visual arc from Moses' viewpoint. | Dr. Falk reads Deuteronomy 34 as a panorama from Mount Nebo/Pisgah rather than a topographical route list. Moses is shown looking across a broad arc from the northern territories through Judah, Jericho, the Negev, and toward Zoar, so the sequence follows a visual sweep from a high viewpoint rather than adjacent places on a map. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=1572" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:12</a> | Could Dr. Falk see an aurora, and how does that relate to Joshua's long day? | He says he saw only a gray smudge through city light pollution, then ties the Joshua reference to an astronomical/calendar discussion. | He says the aurora he saw in the city was only a gray, faintly pink smudge because light pollution and weather made the sky hard to observe. He then separates that from Joshua 10, saying Joshua's long day is not an aurora, Carrington event, or solar eclipse, but omen language comparable to Assyrian omen material about the moon rising with the sun and whether a time is favorable for battle. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=1744" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:04</a> | Would there be an aurora in Egypt? | He says that is unlikely because Egypt is too close to the equator for ordinary auroral visibility. | He says ordinary auroral visibility in Egypt is effectively not expected because Egypt is much closer to the equator than auroral regions. He allows only an extreme event strong enough to damage the power grid as a possible exception, but says Aswan instead offers spectacular dark-sky views of stars and the Milky Way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=1960" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">32:40</a> | If a firstborn heir dies before his father but leaves a son, does the son inherit or does a brother inherit? | He says the brother normally becomes the next heir as the nearest inheriting male. | Dr. Falk explains the rule as proximity to the living progenitor who owns the wealth. If the firstborn son dies, the grandson is a more distant relation than the deceased son's brother, so the inheritance normally moves to the brother; he contrasts this with later British-style succession where a grandson can remain next in line. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=2095" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:55</a> | Should Judges 4:4 be translated as Deborah leading Israel or judging Israel? | He says the key Hebrew verb is "judging," while also describing Deborah as a prophetess. | He points to the verb `shafat`, noting that its range can include judging and, by extension, leading. In context, Deborah sits under her palm tree and Israelites come to her for judgment, so the immediate activity is dispute arbitration; that can become a kind of leadership because her judgments set precedents for the community. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=2409" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:09</a> | Is Deborah the one judge who is never rebuked and only praised in Scripture? | He says she is a good judge and is not rebuked, but she is not unique in that respect. | He says Deborah is indeed presented positively and is not rebuked, but she is not the only judge without rebuke. Other good judges, such as Ehud in his example, also receive no rebuke, while the book still distinguishes them from later bad judges. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=2470" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:10</a> | Do bad judges such as Jephthah and Samson contradict Hebrews 11:33-34 and 11:38? | He says Hebrews 11 should not be treated as simple praise of every listed person's whole life. | Dr. Falk argues that Hebrews 11 is often misread as a simple "hall of faith" praising every named person's character. He says the passage is about people acting on faith in relation to a promise they did not fully receive, and Hebrews then turns the reader's focus to Jesus as better than all of them, including flawed figures such as Samson or Jephthah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=2892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:12</a> | Is Korah's reasoning in Numbers 16:3 analogous to egalitarian use of Galatians 3:28? | He says the two arguments are not directly analogous and need to be kept in their own contexts. | He distinguishes Galatians 3:28, which he says concerns access to salvation in Christ, from Numbers 16, where Korah is using pious language as a pretense to challenge Aaron's priesthood. He later clarifies that he has not seen egalitarians use Galatians 3:28 in the same way Korah uses Numbers 16, though he leaves open that a specific argument would need to be judged on its own terms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=3401" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:41</a> | Which ancient peoples used a seven-day week? | He says the seven-day cycle comes out of Semitic culture, unlike Egyptian ten-day and Roman eight-day systems. | Dr. Falk says the seven-day week appears to come from Semitic culture. He contrasts it with the Egyptian ten-day week and Roman eight-day week, then lists Assyrians, Babylonians, and Canaanites as having seven-day cycles, making Israel's retention of a seven-day week unsurprising in an originally Amorite/Semitic setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=3645" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:45</a> | What are Dr. Falk's thoughts on Niebuhr? | He frames Niebuhr through German dialectic and says that method can be hard for North American readers to follow. | He describes Niebuhr as working in a German dialectical style that presents extremes and expects the reader to find the middle path. Because modern North American readers tend to isolate statements and want direct conclusions, Dr. Falk says this style is difficult and can lead to extreme misreadings if passages are quoted outside their dialectical context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=3931" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:31</a> | How should readers understand the numbers in Numbers 1? | He says scholars agree the census numbers are not being used in the modern straightforward way, but the precise interpretation remains disputed. | He explains that the Hebrew writes the figures out in terms such as `elef` and hundreds, and `elef` may refer to clans, military units, commanders, thousands, or other groupings. He says almost everyone who has worked seriously on the passage agrees the numbers are not functioning exactly like modern arithmetic totals, but there are many competing interpretations and no decisive parallel text yet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=4336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:12:16</a> | Did the Assyrians deport all of northern Israel or mainly the upper class? | He says Assyrian deportation aimed at breaking up clans rather than following the later Babylonian elite-focused pattern. | He says Assyrian deportation was neither total removal nor simply an upper-class exile. Unlike the Babylonians, who targeted elites, the Assyrians tried to prevent rebellion by breaking clan structures: they kept nuclear families together but moved families around the empire and mixed populations from different regions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=4770" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:30</a> | Why do so many Europeans claim that they are descended from the Israelites? | He connects the claim to later Jewish dispersal after the Roman suppression of revolts, not to the Assyrian deportation itself. | Dr. Falk says some European descent claims are better connected to Roman actions after the Jewish revolts, especially the removal of Jews from Jerusalem and its environs, rather than to the lost ten tribes. In his framing, the Assyrian-deported northern Israelites largely became part of Assyrian society or remained in the land and contributed to Samaritan identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=4935" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:15</a> | Is there reason to think some members of the northern tribes fled to Judah during the Assyrian conquest? | He says yes, at least some probably did even though the Assyrian sweep was still severe. | He says there is warrant for believing some northern Israelites fled south because people normally flee during conquest. However, he qualifies that the Assyrian advance was fast and severe, so he would not imagine a mass escape of the northern tribes into Judah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=4986" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:06</a> | Which ancient Near Eastern nations were made up of city-states? | He says essentially all of them were at some point, depending on the stage of nation formation. | He frames city-states as part of recurring nation formation and dissolution. Empires and kingdoms could coalesce from city-states and later fragment back into them, citing Mitanni becoming Aramean city-states and noting similar phases among Canaanites, Hittites, and even later Germany as an analogy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=5354" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:14</a> | Can Dr. Falk elaborate on baptism and communion not being solely symbolic acts? | He explains communion through gathered presence and covenantal participation rather than mere symbolism. | He describes a communal view: where two or more are gathered in Christ's name, Christ is present among them, so communion and baptism are more than bare symbols without requiring transubstantiation or consubstantiation. Baptism is also public witness before the community and, in his account, before the Holy Spirit present among gathered believers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=5784" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:24</a> | How should Christians react to false prophets or teachers who attack the faith? | He says Christians should take the danger seriously and respond by Christ's own principles rather than mere tolerance. | He appeals to Jesus' command to be innocent as doves and wise as serpents. Christians should still be loving and kind toward unbelievers, but he warns that misplaced tolerance can become enabling when it gives harmful teachers more opportunity to damage believers; wisdom means recognizing what such people are doing and not handing them leverage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=6383" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:46:23</a> | Did Joseph replace the current vizier? | He says probably; viziers were not lifetime appointments and could be replaced. | He says it is conceivable and likely that Joseph replaced an existing vizier because the office was held at the king's pleasure, not for life by necessity. A competent vizier might remain for life, but replacement was part of how the job worked, and Joseph himself could also later have been replaced. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=6453" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:47:33</a> | How should we understand human sacrifice in Egypt? | He says the subject is controversial and many once-proposed examples have been discounted as scholarship has developed. | He says older claims have been narrowed sharply. Retainer sacrifice around First and Second Dynasty royal burials has largely been discredited as normal burial, leaving only very thin evidence for possible foundation sacrifices of prisoners under temples, such as a bound skeleton under the Mut temple at Karnak; even that case remains uncertain, so human sacrifice was rare at best. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=6624" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:50:24</a> | What does Dr. Falk think of Jordan Peterson's view of Christianity and the Bible? | He says he is not impressed and describes Peterson's approach as Jungian rather than a strong account of Christianity. | He says Peterson's Christian language often becomes Jungian or psychological "word salad" and misses that the Bible presents God intruding into history, not merely mythological constructs. He finds Peterson's appeal as nominal Christian practice culturally interesting, but characterizes it as a pseudo-religion with a parasitic relationship to Christianity rather than a robust Christian account. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=8301" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:18:21</a> | Are the sons of Korah mentioned in Psalm 42 likely descendants of the Korah from Numbers 16? | He says the title likely reflects a Korahite group, but he distinguishes it from assuming a simple direct link to the rebellion figure. | Dr. Falk warns against assuming one name always means one person. He distinguishes the Korah of Exodus 6, who is already deceased by the Exodus, from the clan chief Korah who dies in Numbers 16, and says the sons of Korah in Psalm 42 are related to the Exodus 6 figure rather than directly to the rebel of Numbers 16. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=8914" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:34</a> | What are Dr. Falk's thoughts on the Gavin and Trent slavery debate? | He says the debate fallout developed as he expected and uses it to criticize how some critics frame objections. | He says the aftermath followed his prediction: critics used the debate to claim their central objections had not been answered and to mock the Christian participants afterward. In his view, the exchange did not advance the central question of whether the Bible endorses slavery and instead illustrated why Christians need to be wise about opponents' tactics. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=9206" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:26</a> | How should the appearance of Asher or Isher in Egyptian topographical lists be explained? | He says topographical lists normally name places rather than tribes, so the identification needs caution. | He says Egyptian topographical lists usually name places, often small settlements, and only rarely tribal groups. He also warns that ancient names were reused constantly, so an Asher-like or Dan-like name appearing in an earlier place list should not automatically be treated as a biblical tribal reference or as evidence for redaction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=10755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:59:15</a> | What symbolism did the plagues represent? | He begins answering through the plague narratives and their symbolic force, with cross-discussion from Hebrews. | He resists treating each plague as a one-to-one symbol. From an Egyptian framework, he says the plagues as a whole communicate social and cosmic disorder: the order of Egypt is turned upside down because of Pharaoh's bad leadership and refusal to release Israel. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=10977" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:57</a> | Did the Nile literally become blood in the first plague? | He leans against literal blood and prefers blood-like appearance or properties within ancient idiom. | He says he does not think the river literally became blood. In ancient Near Eastern idiom, blood on the ground or in water is black or dark rather than fresh red, so he reads the plague as water that looked and perhaps smelled like blood, not as chemically transformed blood. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=11196" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:36</a> | What are the meanings of other names listed in Numbers 16? | He says many of the names are uncertain, so only a few can be discussed with confidence. | He says many Numbers 16 names cannot be explained confidently. He offers a few examples, such as Abiram meaning something like "exalted father" and Eliab meaning "God is father," but stresses that biblical names are not always recoverable and many meanings remain uncertain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=11320" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:08:40</a> | Why was Hatshepsut not mentioned in the early-date Exodus playlist, and how should one answer the claim that Senenmut was Moses? | He says Senenmut's Egyptian parents and other evidence make the identification with Moses untenable. | He says Senenmut cannot be Moses because Senenmut's Egyptian parents are known from tomb evidence, and his role as Hatshepsut's courtier and royal nurse does not fit the Moses story. He also says Hatshepsut is not central to the Exodus narrative in his reconstruction and that forcing her into Moses' adoption creates geographical and historical problems. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=12094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:34</a> | What references should someone use for slavery by biblical scholars? | He says Richard Averbeck has written on the subject and recommends starting with good commentaries and bibliographies. | He says he does not keep up with slavery literature because it is a specialization with too much annual output to track casually. He names Richard Averbeck as someone who has written on the subject and recommends beginning with good commentaries and bibliography lists, such as those compiled by the Gospel Coalition, before moving deeper into the specialist literature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=12192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:12</a> | What kinds of favors could someone ask from a pharaoh? | He says people might ask for a job or honorific status, but not vulgar material favors. | Dr. Falk says suitable requests include a job with a title, honorific status such as "friend of the king," or administrative offices like supervisor of works. These are status and responsibility requests; material gifts could be given by the king, but they should not be demanded. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=12378" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:26:18</a> | Why not ask the king for stuff? | He says asking for material goods would be viewed as vulgar and could offend the king. | He says asking Pharaoh for objects, food, clothing, a home, or similar goods would be low-class and offensive because such provisions belonged under officials and staff. A king could voluntarily reward someone lavishly, as in the Sinuhe example or the chain of gold, but that generosity had to come from the king's initiative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=12544" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:04</a> | Could the king arbitrarily give someone the death penalty? | He says no; even without written law, Egyptian society had legal traditions that constrained the king. | He says Egyptian kings were constrained by legal tradition even without written law codes, so arbitrary execution would violate ancestral norms and delegitimize the king. A ruler might frame someone or use power wrongly, but that would be treated as a bad-omen, line-crossing act rather than normal royal authority. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=12706" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:46</a> | Are there comprehensive commentaries on the historicity of Daniel in its cultural context? | He recommends the Tyndale House essay volume on problems in Daniel and names Wiseman, Fairman, and Kitchen as contributors. | He recalls the Tyndale House volume on problems in Daniel, edited by Donald Wiseman and including contributors such as Fairman and Kitchen, as a useful but dated and hard-to-find resource. He also says readers should check commentaries, and he personally supports Daniel's historicity while adding that a later date would not automatically destroy its scriptural status. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=12876" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:34:36</a> | Could Dr. Falk explain the synchronism of Neferhotep I and Hammurabi as "Kish"? | He says there is not much for a full video, but points to the relevant stela and citation trail. | He says the evidence is basically one synchronism, probably not enough for a full video. The key point is a three-way link: a royal gift ties Neferhotep I to Zimri-Lim of Mari, while a nearby stela mentions Zimri-Lim and Hammurabi, tying Dynasty 13 Egypt to the First Dynasty of Babylon in a way that fits other evidence from Avaris. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=13758" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:18</a> | Is Richard Dawkins essentially a Christian now? | He says no; liking some trappings of Christian culture does not make Dawkins a Christian. | Dr. Falk says Dawkins is not a Christian and remains an atheist. He may prefer the cultural trappings of Christian society over some alternatives, but appreciating Christian cultural inheritance does not amount to Christian faith. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=13806" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:06</a> | What was the economy like around the time of Moses? | He describes it as a barter economy where metals were weighed and many people were paid in food or tangible goods. | He says there was no coinage, so metals such as silver functioned by weight, and most exchange was barter. Ordinary laborers and farmers were paid primarily in grain or food rations, while artisans might also receive dried fish, wine, or, for harder or more skilled work, beef. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=13943" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:23</a> | What are Dr. Falk's thoughts on the Oxford Annotated Study Bible? | He says he has not used it and does not comment seriously on a Bible without living with a physical copy. | He says he does not know the Oxford Annotated Study Bible well enough to comment because he has not lived with a physical copy. His general principle is that Bible translations and study Bibles should be evaluated after sustained use, except in rare cases that are obviously bad at first glance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=14901" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:08:21</a> | What are Dr. Falk's thoughts on the claim that Christianity leads to nihilism? | He says that is a distortion of Christianity. | He says Christianity only leads to nihilism if someone badly distorts Christian doctrine. He mentions that figures such as Nietzsche need to be read in dialectical context and rejects the idea that predestination or other doctrines, properly understood, imply that nothing matters. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=15654" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:20:54</a> | What does Dr. Falk think of Esoterica's claims about Israel being polytheistic and Yahweh being the son of El? | He says he has not watched the channel, affirms Israel often practiced polytheism, and distinguishes that from the underlying theology. | He says he has not watched Esoterica enough to evaluate the channel directly. On the substance, he can affirm that many Israelites practiced polytheism, but he distinguishes that historical practice from the theology the biblical texts present and would not collapse Israel's religion into the claim without careful qualification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/2f1Teze-G7Y?t=16413" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:33:33</a> | How should artists accurately describe Satan? | He says Satan should be portrayed as destructive, jealous, and already defeated rather than as a misunderstood ruler. | He says Satan should not be romanticized as a misunderstood ruler with dignity or a crown. Artists should present him as jealous, destructive, parasitic, and contemptible: a liar and destroyer who ruins what belongs to others rather than a tragic hero or legitimate king. |
